title: Ransomware & Backup Awareness
description: Test your ability to recognize ransomware risks, understand the value of backups, and respond safely.

choices.text: A software update tool
A cyberattack that locks, encrypts, or makes data and systems unusable
A type of password manager
A secure backup method

questions.explanation: Ransomware is a cyberattack that can block access to files or systems and disrupt business operations.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a common way ransomware gets into an organization?

choices.text: Clicking a phishing link or attachment
Reading a printed invoice
Restarting a computer
Using a company-approved calendar

questions.explanation: Phishing emails and unsafe attachments are common ways ransomware enters organizations.

questions.prompt: It is safe to assume backups will always be available after a ransomware attack.

choices.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Backups may also be damaged, encrypted, deleted, or otherwise unavailable if they are not properly protected.

questions.prompt: Why are backups important in a ransomware incident?

choices.text: They make phishing emails easier to detect
They can help the organization recover files and systems after disruption
They automatically stop all malware
They remove the need for reporting incidents

questions.explanation: Backups can support recovery, but only if they are regularly maintained, tested, and protected.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains why ransomware is a business problem and not just an IT problem?

choices.text: It only affects antivirus settings
It can interrupt payroll, sales, scheduling, customer service, and normal operations
It only matters for remote workers
It only affects servers, not people

questions.explanation: Ransomware can stop important business functions across the organization, not just technical systems.

questions.prompt: A protected backup strategy is part of cybersecurity.

choices.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Backups are a key part of cyber resilience because they help reduce damage when prevention fails.

questions.prompt: Scenario: You click a file from an unexpected email, and a shared folder suddenly becomes inaccessible. What should you do first?

choices.text: Wait to see if the issue fixes itself
Keep opening files to test what still works
Report it immediately and follow your organization's response process
Send the file to coworkers to ask if they can open it

questions.explanation: Unusual file lockouts or sudden access problems should be reported immediately to reduce damage and spread.

questions.prompt: Which of the following makes a backup strategy stronger?

choices.text: Never testing the backups
Keeping backups exposed to the same risks as the main system
Regularly backing up data and protecting backup access
Letting every employee change backup settings

questions.explanation: Good backups are regular, protected, and tested so they can actually support recovery.

questions.prompt: What lesson does the Ohio example in this module reinforce?

choices.text: Ransomware only affects government offices
Only very large national companies face ransomware risk
Organizations in Ohio can face serious operational disruption from ransomware
Backups always prevent service interruptions

questions.explanation: The Ohio example shows that ransomware can seriously disrupt real organizations close to home, including their day-to-day operations and services.

questions.prompt: What should an employee avoid doing during a suspected ransomware incident?

choices.text: Reporting the problem quickly
Following the organization's response instructions
Hiding the issue or delaying reporting
Stopping interaction with suspicious files

questions.explanation: Hiding or delaying reports can allow ransomware to spread further and make recovery harder.
